speaker: interviewer
text: Today is September 30 in 1996. We're continuing with the interview of Joyce Lipper. This is tape number two. We were talking about when you were on the road leaving your town. So we're going to back up for a minute, before we continue. I wanted to ask you a little bit more about what the Jewish committee did, besides helping arrange for food and whatever, and what you knew of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community also tried to deal with the Germans. They used to request a certain amount of people for work every day. It happened that one of the German soldiers got killed. So they will come to the community, and they will ask for a certain amount of people, and they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were-- did-- who were they selected, the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that that was a very varied thing. They used to ask for volunteers. But when people find out that you going to your death, people didn't want to go. But we also had incidents where a father would go for his son, knowing that he's gonna be killed. But he would go for his son, for his young son, or for his daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to take them away. You never knew where you're going or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they report to? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the German authorities in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see these people going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people know they were going to their death originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not originally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they think they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: They thought-- I mean, they told them that they're going-- they're taking them to work. They used to tell them that they taked them to work, that they're-- they're sending them to Germany to work. But were-- those people never came back. So after a while, you knew what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any members of the Jewish committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I mean, when I can think back, they were all from the town that everybody knew each other. So it wasn't a big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they picked to be a member of this committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess you had to form a committee that somebody should represent everybody, to deal with things. So a lot of people were just nominated, or they thought that they'll-- everybody thought that they'll be a-- do a good job working on our behalf with the Germans. So those are the people that tried to belong and do things for the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the head? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what other kinds of things did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They negotiated. Let's say that, when they used to ask for a certain amount of people, they tried to negotiate not to give them as many, or to give them maybe older people, or to be able to exchange, if they caught some people. They used to catch people in the street, if they wanted them, and take them away. So a lot of times, it happened that they tried to negotiate-- they should let them out, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think they were doing a good job?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I didn't think so. But now, I think that, under the circumstances, they did the best they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That there was favoritism. And-- and they tried to-- for their own mode and for people from town. But look here, that's-- that's life. That's life. Now I look at things different. Because eventually, they got killed too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it that changed your mind about what their job was?

speaker: interviewee
text: What changed my mind? I grew up. I saw life and how-- what it takes to live in this world and how to live. And I realized that people just have to do what they have to do. I-- at that time, I wasn't knowledgeable about outside world and how things would go on. And you-- everybody tried to help themselves or save themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you were preparing to leave with your boyfriend. Did you have a discussion with your mother about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember it good, that I came and told my mother that I want to go with Eli, not with them. And my mother said to me, kids, where are you going by yourself? You're gonna be killed on the road. And we told her that we're gonna be killed anyway, so we might as well try to help ourselves. And my mother said that, if you want to go away with him, you have to-- he has to marry you. You don't go away with a man just like that. And we agreed to get married. And there was an elderly man by us in town. And he acted as a rabbi and gave us [YIDDISH], like you say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The marriage vows.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you were-- that was a Jewish ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't much of a-- a ceremony, just that he pronounced us man and wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the 40-- I-- about the beginning of '42, or end of '41. I don't think exactly remember, but I always considered that my-- we got married in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your mother there when you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it took place in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hidden? Or could you do this out in the open?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was not-- there was no place to do it. I mean, we didn't have a place of our own. We just did it because of my mother and his mother. My husband didn't have a father either. His father passed away before. So because they wanted it, so we did it. And he went to his house, and I went to my house where I was, until it came the time to go away together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that decided when you would leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband had four brothers. And they had a business in-- in town by us. And they were involved with a lot of people. And they had connections. They knew things. Like if something would happen, they knew. They were told about it. And-- and they knew, at that time, that something is gonna happen, that they gonna make our town free from Jews, and that it's time to go. And we were just waiting for the time when they'll tell us to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you prepared to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I-- not a thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever I had on my back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you set out on the road, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you traveled at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My husband's oldest brother-- we came from work that night, and he said, you have to go. We have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people went?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's three brothers with their wives. Their mother didn't go. She didn't want to go. She says she's not going. Whatever will be with everybody will be with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a plan where you were going to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I didn't, but my husband's family had a plan that we're gonna go in to the Rowne ghetto, and we'll stay there and see what's gonna happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us a couple days, because we traveled to in-- at night. And you had to try to get into the city where nobody will see you. And when we did come into the city, we had to go into the-- I forgot the word-- to the camp, you know, that we had in Rowne to the ghetto, that we shouldn't be seen. And you are just as afraid of Jewish people that, you know, might not want you there, that they-- they always were afraid that outsiders would start trouble or do harm for them. And so you had to go in and try to mix in with the crowd, without being noticed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took a couple days, and we were-- I think, one night-- it was the second night when we were in the city, that we got in, and the following day we went to work with everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got in where? To a house? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to-- to a place where they took us in. That mean we had people that we knew, and they took us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were traveling there, what did you do during the day, if you were traveling at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were trying to hide in the field, under the straw, under bushes, under whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever we could get on the road. A lot of times, at night, we would stop in some kind of village and knock on a door and ask for bread, for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. Sometimes. Yes, but you couldn't stay there because there were incidents that, when you ask for food, they'll give it to you, but they'll send the authorities on you. So you had take and go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, what were the conditions like in Rowne then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, a lot of people in one room. There, [? well, ?] the ghetto was guarded by German soldiers. You went out [? and ?] during the day to work and came at night. You had to-- they made sure that you came and you stayed inside. And were you-- to get that little piece of bread, or the little soup, whatever you got to eat, you had to go to work. We went to work in a sugar factory. And at least we had sugar that we could eat. You couldn't-- you couldn't take it out, but you can eat it there, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work-- What was the work? What kind of work did you do in the sugar factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: We cut the [? beads. ?] We washed the [? beads. ?] All kinds of work. Worked with machinery, all kinds of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the workers? Just the Jews from in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish people, but there was some non-Jewish people. Yes. But they were people that came to work there, I would say, voluntarily, and used to get paid, you know, or maybe worked there before. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you did you have any contact with these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. Not in that town. We didn't know anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a Jewish committee in that town as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their function?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew very little about it, but it was the function to deal with the Germans, you know, that whatever they wanted, try to provide them with that they should be satisfied, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people being sent out from there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sent out to-- to work?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, not to work. Did-- did people leave from the ghetto there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: They always used to come and go, run away. From different towns people came, tried to get in. They was always doing something, but it wasn't like open things. You didn't know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there roundups by the Germans there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did those take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very often. At night, they'll come and knock on the door. They used to take young girls. And lot of times, they came back. And most of the time, they didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do anything special to keep from being taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not really. You couldn't do much, unless, at night, when they knocked on the doors, you used to run up to the attic and hide, or hide somewhere, wherever you could, that you shouldn't be seen, so you'll stay safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to communicate with your mother at all at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time, I didn't know anything about my mother and my sister, until about three months later when things were getting very bad there. I decided, with my husband at that time, to go to Alexandria where I had aunts and uncles and a grandmother from on my mother's side. And we decided to go see what's doing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the fall of '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. '42. Right. In the fall of '42. Yeah. And we made it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just myself and my husband. And we were walking. And the non-Jewish people that-- the farmers saw us. And used to-- they said that I'm a Czeska-- I didn't look Jewish-- and I-- and I take a Jew somewhere, because my husband looked Jewish a lot, more than I did. They recognized us. And they asked us where we're going. And we said that we are looking for family. And we came to my grandmother's house there. And I found my mother and my sister there.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long was that trip? How long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couple days, as I say, because you didn't walk all the time. A couple days. Yeah. And you didn't also go with the main roads. You tried to go through the village, and you know, and all. And we stayed in Alexandria. We could-- at the time, when we came there, they didn't let you work already. It was also a smaller town than Rowne. And they didn't let us go to work. It was just a ghetto. And you used to get a certain amount of rationing, bread and a little soup once a day. And we knew that it's not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the days?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day? A lot of times, there were little jobs that they used to take you for do-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like to clean. Oh, to-- they used to do laundry for the German soldiers there. And we used to do that. I had a chance to work in a kitchen. And I never saw a whole chicken in my life. And they put me in the kitchen. And we were cooking. We're cleaning the chickens. And I put in the whole chicken in the pot to cook, without taking out the area where the chicken keeps its food. And when I put in the chicken to cook and the food from the chicken that I didn't take out that part started to came up-- come up in the soup. And I got very scared that I-- they're gonna tell me that I want to poison them or something. And they're going to kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was a German soldier. And he hollered at me. He says, what did you do? I says, I've never seen a chicken. I didn't know that you have to take it out and so on. And he came over and patted me on my shoulder and told me, that's all right, child. And he let me get away with it. And I went there in the next day to work again.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first time in my life in-- that I had an incident with a German soldier. And it was-- he has showed a little bit of kindness in him. A-- a human being. And we-- we didn't stay too long there, because we saw that it's coming to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe two or three months. And when we left our town, all the brothers-- that four brothers made up that, if something will happen, there is one chance that we have to go back to-- to our town and try to come to that lady that wanted to hire us, to save us from the Germans. And that was my husband's brother's girlfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [? Walla ?] at that time. Her name now is Bella.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how had she said she had wanted to hide you? When was that? You knew about this before you left your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She wanted her friend, that my-- my husband's brother, she wanted him to do it right away, not to run away. But he said no. He didn't want to go. He said he wants to go with the family. But they had a lot of their valuable things by her. And they trusted her. And she said that, if something comes up, you should know that you always come back here. And she wasn't Jewish. She had brothers that were in the Ukraine in underground against Jews, against the Germans, against the Russians. And they knew that she had a Jewish boyfriend. But it's their sister, so they accepted her back to the family and tried to protect her, because she said that her boyfriend is gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know she was planning to hide him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it that she was planning to hide him?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in a small, little town. It's not a town. I would say a village. And they had a small house not far from the railroad track. And she lived there with her mother and three brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowostav.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the one we talked about earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And after, when we were in Alexandria, and we saw that it's not good, and there is no place anymore to go, my husband wanted to go there. He still had some money on him, but we didn't know the way, how to go from Alexandria there. Not to-- there wasn't-- you couldn't go with transportation, so we were looking for somebody to take us there. And there was a elderly non-Jewish man that said that he knows back ways. And he'll go with us and take us there for money. And my husband gave him the rest of the money that he had on him, and we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how much was that? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a lot of money?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was golden coins. At that time, that's what you had with you. You couldn't have Russian money or any kind of money, because you didn't know where you were, if you were under the German or under the Russian. But the gold was always good. My mother thought that we are crazy kids of going. I wanted my sister to come with us, but she said no. She says she's going to stay. Whatever will happen with everybody will happen with-- to her. And I-- my husband said, we're gonna get killed here, so we'll get killed on the road. At least we not gonna try. And we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old was your sister at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was about four years older than I was. We left. And when we came to Nowostov, the place where my sister-in-law was-- not my sister-in-law at that time-- but-- we stayed in a field on the hay. And the man went to her. We-- we gave him the address, and he went to talk to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna stop here to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Oh, god. Who will want to--
